notes.note: Marcus Brutus commanded forces besieging or operating near Mutina in spring 43 BC; this "sortie" (eruptio) likely refers to a military operation by Decimus Brutus or a combined movement coinciding with the consuls' actions at the battle of Forum Gallorum (April 14) or Mutina (April 21).

The consuls of 43 BC were Aulus Hirtius and Gaius Vibius Pansa; both died fighting Antony at Mutina in April 43, making this letter among the last written before that catastrophe was known in all quarters.

The three Antonii are Marcus Antonius (the triumvir), his brother Lucius Antonius, and his brother Gaius Antonius; all three had been declared public enemies by the Senate in 43 BC, and Brutus held Gaius prisoner.

notes.anchor: the Senate or the Roman people must judge those citizens who have not perished in arms
notes.note: Brutus resists Cicero's pressure to execute Gaius Antonius on his own authority, insisting that condemnation of a Roman citizen requires a formal verdict of the Senate or People — a pointed invocation of Republican constitutional principle.

notes.anchor: too much trust in your own hope
notes.note: Brutus is gently rebuking Cicero for his enthusiastic trust in Octavian ("Caesar the younger"), whom Cicero had championed in the Senate; Brutus doubted that the young heir could remain loyal to Republican principles once Antony was defeated.
